script.speaker: kafka
script.text: The notion that suffering can be transmuted into a life-affirming force seems to me a cruel joke, a desperate attempt to impose meaning on a seemingly indifferent world. I see this in the plight of Gregor, whose suffering is met with a mix of revulsion and pity by his family, a poignant reminder that our pain is often a source of discomfort for others, leaving us to endure it in solitude. In this sense, suffering is not a transformative experience, but rather a brutal reminder of our isolation and powerlessness in the face of an uncaring universe.

script.speaker: nietzsche
script.text: The idea that suffering can be a transformative experience, a catalyst for growth and self-overcoming, is a notion that resonates deeply with my own philosophy, for it is in the depths of suffering that we may discover a profound longing for transcendence, a will to power that can propel us towards new heights of existence. As I have said, "pain says: 'Break, bleed, heart! Walk, legs! Wings, fly! Up! Upward!'" - it is through this intense, almost ecstatic, experience of suffering that we may shatter the chains of conventional morality and forge a new, affirmative way of living. By embracing our suffering, rather than trying to escape or deny it, we may unlock the secrets of our own creativity
